query: You are working on the ankidroid/Anki-Android repository. The codebase is at version 2.186 (commit: d6dd8b51). ## Issue to Resolve [BUG]: Can't display images with `#` in filenames ### Checked for duplicates? - [X] This issue is not a duplicate ### What are the steps to reproduce this bug? 1. Add a card including an image file containing `#` (hashtag) in its filename --- --- --- --- 3. Show the card in reviewer or previewer ### Expected behaviour The image is shown, as on 2.16.5. (2.16.5) ### Actual behaviour The broken-image icon is shown, and the image isn't shown. ### Debug info ```text AnkiDroid Version = 2.17.6 (568538896be943818ca793dd2635822265712def) Backend Version = 0.1.34-anki23.12.1 (23.12.1 1a1d4d5419c6b57ef3baf99c9d2d9cf85d36ae0a) Android Version = 11 (SDK 30) ProductFlavor = play Manufacturer = OPPO Model = CPH2013 Hardware = qcom Webview User Agent = Mozilla/5.0 (Linux; Android 11; CPH2013 Build/RKQ1.200903.002; wv) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Version/4.0 Chrome/122.0.6261.119 Mobile Safari/537.36 ACRA UUID = df169a4d-29ec-46b6-b114-141ca7e01ba3 Crash Reports Enabled = true ``` ### (Optional) Anything else you want to share? - Originally reported in Anki Forum: https://forums.ankiweb.net/t/ankiweb-and-ankidroid-do-not-display-images-containing-pound-hashtag-sharp-symbol/42444/1 and dae has commented there. - As reported there, this issue occurs on AnkiWeb, too. ![image](https://github.com/ankidroid/Anki-Android/assets/10436072/c588f585-b23c-4be6-b8f1-8a098a127705) So, strictly speaking, this is not a bug specific to AnkiDroid, but some approach specific to AnkiDroid may be needed, I'm not sure though. - This issue doesn't occur on Anki Desktop (as reported in Anki Forum) and on AnkiMobile. - The `#` is shown as `%23` on the editor of Anki Desktop (as reported in Anki Forum). ![image](https://github.com/ankidroid/Anki-Android/assets/10436072/5ba75763-6007-4668-b036-0cb13e54cdc5) > \> Windows changes it back to a # in the sort field, but the HTML text remains unchanged > > This happens because Anki stores filenames without encoding on disk (legacy reasons), but needs to encode them before they’re shown in the editor. (dae) ### Research - [X] I am reporting a bug specific to AnkiDroid (Android app) - [X] I have checked the [manual](https://ankidroid.org/docs/manual.html) and the [FAQ](https://github.com/ankidroid/Anki-Android/wiki/FAQ) and could not find a solution to my issue - [X] (Optional) I have confirmed the issue is not resolved in the latest alpha release ([instructions](https://docs.ankidroid.org/manual.html#betaTesting)) (2.18alpha6) ## Additional Context from Issue Discussion It seems to have already been fixed in the current HEAD. I don't think so, it is ongoing discussion in the PR, https://github.com/ankidroid/Anki-Android/pull/15909#issuecomment-2001940011 Thank you for the feedback. Not for objection (since I don't have enough knowledge about the topic), just for rewording more accurately and for what it is worth, the issue seems to have been fixed in my debug version build from a854e93 (Mar 17, 2024). On the other hand, a card with the image file added in Anki Desktop is not shown properly (i.e. shown as a broken-image icon) even in the debug version of AnkiDroid. In this case, `#` is shown as it is in the editor and the browser. “#” is a illegal char in HTML, so it’s not supposed to work anywhere if not encoded. Ankidroid encodes it when importing, and AFAIK Anki does it as well. > Ankidroid encodes it when importing In case that users import cards that contain image files with unencoded `#`, for example, importing the following shared deck, why images with `#` in filenames are shown in reviewer on 2.16.5, but not on 2.17.6? - https://ankiweb.net/shared/info/789434294 --- Read now damien's comment about "legacy reasons". Since `#` still isn't supposed to be treated as something else that isn't a URL fragment marker in that context, this is a deliberate anki breakage of the URL scheme. I hate breaking things deliberately without a unavoidable reason, but I'll send the fix ## Relevant Files The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue: - `.idea/dictionaries/davidallison.xml` - `AnkiDroid/src/main/java/com/ichi2/libanki/TemplateManager.kt` ## Instructions Please analyze this issue and implement a fix. Make the necessary code changes to resolve the problem described above. Focus on: - Understanding the root cause of the issue - Making minimal, targeted changes to fix the problem - Ensuring your changes don't break existing functionality ## Important Evaluation Notes **Your work will be evaluated by running hidden tests that you do not have access to.** These tests will verify that your fix correctly addresses the issue. You should be confident in your solution before finishing. **If you write tests to verify your changes, create them in NEW test files only** - do NOT modify existing test files ## CRITICAL RESTRICTIONS **NEVER use git commands.** Do not run `git status`, `git diff`, `git checkout`, `git reset`, or ANY other git commands. Using git will corrupt the evaluation environment and invalidate your entire submission. All file operations should be done through normal file editing and reading tools, not git.

content.thinking:
**Fixing image URL encoding**

I need a solution for images with "#" in their filenames. I'm inspecting TemplateManager.kt, which might be rewriting media URLs for webview. The issue arises because, in HTML, "#" denotes a fragment; if a filename contains it and isn't encoded, the browser will truncate the request. I’ll need to encode "#" as "%23" in the src URL. Anki Desktop does encode it, but older decks might not. AnkiDroid needs a way to manage these unencoded references. I’ll review how local media is loaded in webview.

**Identifying parsing issues**

I noticed that when using `parseSourcesToFileScheme`, there's an issue with how it deals with the `#` character in URLs. The `attrValue`, containing `#`, is causing the URI parsing to treat it as a fragment, which leads to incorrect paths. For instance, `src="foo#bar.jpg"` causes the code to yield the path as just "foo," resulting in broken images. To fix this, I need to treat `#` as part of the filename when parsing local file schemes. This means reconstructing the original path by combining the path and fragment correctly.**Clarifying fragment handling**

I noticed that the `#` character indicates a fragment, but I should only treat it as part of the filename for local file references without a scheme. For remote HTTP links, we can skip this because the scheme is non-null. For relative file references, it's important to properly decode the `attrValue` while ensuring there's no XSS risk since it’s just a file path. I want to preserve the `#` part in the filename, even though it's usually not used for image tags. However, I need to clarify how to handle any legitimate fragments in local media references.**Assessing fragment importance**

I realize that for local media stored as files, the fragment (`#`) isn't generally meaningful for images, but it might matter for videos, especially if the filename contains it. My goal is to display media whose filenames include `#`. I should check if the file exists without the fragment, versus if it exists when including `#` and the fragment. 

My algorithm will involve parsing the `attrValue`, determining the relative path, and computing the path with the fragment when necessary. I have to keep in mind how percent encodings work within fragments and that Java’s URI handling might return decoded fragments.**Clarifying URI handling**

I've figured out that the URI stores both raw and decoded values, and `getFragment` returns the decoded version. However, I need to preserve percent sequences in the filename. When building the path, I should interpret percent encoding while keeping in mind that filenames on disk are unencoded. If `attrValue` contains something like `%20`, it's likely part of the filename but might be stored as a space in environments like Anki. 

I'll build a `File(mediaDir, candidate)` and check if it exists. If it doesn't, I'll check if the version with the fragment exists. If there's any invalid encoding related to `#`, I might replace it with `%23`.**Investigating file URI generation**

I’m digging into how the `getFileUri` function generates a file URI using `URI("file", p, null)`. It seems that this function doesn't escape `#` correctly when the path string contains it. If `p` has a `#`, it might break the URI since `URI` expects scheme-specific parts without unescaped `#`. 

I need to ensure that while constructing file URIs, `#` gets percent-encoded properly. If it doesn't, the webview could misinterpret the fragment, leading to incorrect paths. I’m considering running a quick test in Java to check how it parses this issue.

Sometimes, the fragment contains parts of the base name, like "gent.png." However, with media fragments, such as "#t=...", they usually lack a period. I think we might treat a fragment as a filename if it appears to be a file extension or part of a filename. We could reconstruct the filename if the fragment contains a known extension. But it's tricky when the base name has no extension but the fragment does. I need to explore this carefully!**Determining filename with fragments** I'm analyzing the case of filenames like "foo#bar.jpg." Here, the base path "foo" lacks an extension while the fragment "bar.jpg" does. If the `attrUri.path` has no extension but the fragment does, it suggests the filename includes a "#". In contrast, "video.mp4#t=10" is safe because the path has an extension. I think the simplest way to handle this could be to combine the path and fragment whenever the fragment is present. But I should be cautious, especially with cases like "A#B.png," where extensions are involved.**Addressing filename and fragment handling** I'm diving into the case of "A.png#B," where the filename contains a "#" but the fragment has no extension. My heuristic wouldn’t combine them, but a user might need that filename structure. I’m considering treating the raw attribute as a path segment instead, removing any query. To detect the scheme, I might check for ":" before any "/" and handle the URI conditionally. For an encoded filename like `foo%23bar.jpg`, the URI decodes it to "foo#bar.jpg," which refers to the actual file. I want to fix the potential bug with handling unencoded "#". My approach will involve checking if a fragment looks like a file extension and merging it with the path accordingly. If the fragment contains a dot and the path does not, I’ll treat it as a filename continuation. content.text: Implemented a targeted fix in `parseSourcesToFileScheme()` to correctly handle unescaped `#` in local media filenames. ### Root cause `java.net.URI("foo#bar.jpg")` interprets `#bar.jpg` as a *fragment*, so the code previously used only `path="foo"` when resolving the media file, producing an incorrect `file:///.../foo` URL. ### Fix When rewriting `img/video/audio/source` `src` attributes with no scheme: - If the URI has a fragment and it *looks like the filename continuation* (path has no extension, fragment does), we reconstruct the real filename as `path + "#" + fragment` before converting to `file:///...`. - This preserves the correct filename and results in `file:///.../foo%23bar.jpg`. ### Tests Added a new unit test file: - Verifies `` rewrites to `file:///.../foo%23bar.jpg` - Verifies a media fragment like `
